does: This paper introduces ICCU, an in-context continual unlearning framework that addresses sequential unlearning requests without modifying model parameters. Rather than fine-tuning the model for each removal request, ICCU induces interpretable refusal rules from unlearning datasets and applies them at inference time through filtering or system prompts. The key contribution is demonstrating that readable, pattern-based rules can be accumulated cumulatively to suppress specific knowledge while preserving model utility. Experiments validate that ICCU effectively removes target information across sequential requests, maintains model performance on general tasks, and generalizes to paraphrased and cross-lingual variations of unlearning queries.
connects: This work extends the machine unlearning literature, which has primarily focused on parameter-level modifications through fine-tuning or gradient-based methods. ICCU responds to practical limitations in continual unlearning settings where multiple removal requests arrive over time, a scenario inadequately addressed by prior approaches that incur computational costs and utility loss per request. The paper builds on recent interest in in-context learning methods that modify model behavior without weight updates, and connects to research on interpretability and rule-based decision systems by extracting human-readable refusal rules. It also relates to work on inference-time control of language models through prompting and filtering techniques.
enables: This work opens directions for deployment-friendly unlearning pipelines where sequential removal requests can be handled efficiently without retraining. The extracted refusal rules are interpretable and auditable, enabling transparency about what knowledge the model has been instructed to forget—valuable for regulatory compliance and user trust. The compositionality of the approach suggests extensions to complex unlearning scenarios with interdependent requests or to combining unlearning rules with other in-context control mechanisms. Future work could explore rule refinement, handling conflicting unlearning objectives, and applying similar pattern-induction methods to other forms of inference-time model control beyond unlearning.
